01-why-workflows-matter.instruction: List 3 manual workflows in your work or life that are repetitive, error-prone, or time-consuming. For each, describe the current manual process and estimate how much time it wastes per week.
01-why-workflows-matter.placeholder: Workflow 1: Email sorting — Every morning I spend 45 minutes...
01-why-workflows-matter.hint: Think about tasks you do repeatedly that follow a pattern: sorting emails, generating reports, scheduling, data entry, sending follow-ups, categorizing files. The best automation candidates are high-frequency, rule-based, and time-consuming but low-creativity.
01-why-workflows-matter.goodExample: 1. Email triage — Every morning I read 80+ emails, mentally categorize (urgent/important/FYI), and decide who to respond to first. Takes 45 min/day (3.75 hours/week). Error-prone because I sometimes miss urgent messages buried in newsletters. 2. Weekly status reports — Every Friday I manually check Jira, Slack, and GitHub to compile what the team accomplished. Takes 2 hours/week. Error-prone because I miss updates in Slack threads. 3. Invoice processing — I receive 20 vendor invoices/month via email, manually extract amount/date/vendor, enter into accounting spreadsheet. Takes 1 hour/week. Error-prone because I sometimes mistype numbers.
01-why-workflows-matter.successMessage: Identifying pain is step one. These 3 are your first automation targets.

01-why-workflows-matter.instruction: A marketing team manually processes 200 customer emails per day. Each takes 12 minutes (read, categorize, draft response, get approval, send). Analyze: what AI workflow improvements are possible and what's the business impact?
01-why-workflows-matter.placeholder: Current state: 200 emails x 12 min = ... AI improvements: ...
01-why-workflows-matter.hint: Current: 40 person-hours/day (5 FTEs). AI workflow: auto-categorize (saves 2 min), AI-draft responses (saves 5 min), auto-approve routine categories (saves 3 min for 80% of emails). New time: ~11 hours/day. Savings: 29 hours/day = $733/day at $25/hour = ~$190K/year. AI costs: ~$50/month.
01-why-workflows-matter.goodExample: 40 person-hours/day breaks into 4 automatable steps: (1) Classification — AI categorizes instantly (6.7 hours saved). (2) Response drafting — AI generates context-aware drafts (16.7 hours saved). (3) Approval routing — auto-approve 80% routine emails (8 hours saved). (4) Sending — automated trigger. Net: 40 down to 11 person-hours/day. $190K/year labor savings against $600/year API costs. Team shifts from email processing to relationship building.
01-why-workflows-matter.successMessage: Powerful workflow analysis. The biggest AI workflow wins come from eliminating repetitive classification and drafting.

02-mapping-your-processes.instruction: Pick one painful workflow and map it step by step. Include the trigger event, each action, every decision point (if/then), and the end result. Use arrows (->) between steps.
02-mapping-your-processes.placeholder:
Trigger: New customer email arrives ->
Step 1: Read email ->
Decision: Is it a complaint? If yes ->...
02-mapping-your-processes.hint: A good process map reveals hidden complexity. Most workflows have 2-3 decision points people don't realize they're making. Include: what triggers the workflow, every step in order, every decision (if X then Y, else Z), where the result ends up, and how long each step takes.
02-mapping-your-processes.badExample: Email comes in, I deal with it, done.
02-mapping-your-processes.goodExample: Trigger: New support email arrives -> Step 1: Read email (2 min) -> Decision: Is it about billing? If yes -> Route to finance team. If no -> Step 2: Categorize (bug report, feature request, how-to question) (1 min) -> Decision: Is it urgent? If yes -> Respond within 1 hour. If no -> Add to queue. -> Step 3: Draft response using template (5 min) -> Decision: Does it need manager approval? If yes -> Forward to manager, wait (avg 4 hours). If no -> Step 4: Send response -> Step 5: Update ticket status in CRM (1 min) -> End. Total: 9 min active + up to 4 hours waiting.
02-mapping-your-processes.successMessage: Process mapping reveals hidden complexity and automation opportunities — especially those invisible wait times.

02-mapping-your-processes.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to create a visual process map (in text format) for a workflow with decision branches. Include the process, the decision criteria, and who's involved at each step.
02-mapping-your-processes.placeholder: Help me map this workflow with decision branches...
02-mapping-your-processes.hint: You are a process mapping expert. Create a flowchart in ASCII/text format for my customer onboarding workflow. Steps: signup, referral check, plan selection (Free/Pro/Enterprise), payment processing, welcome email, check-in sequence. Show decision diamonds, process boxes, and arrows. Mark which steps an AI agent could handle vs. which need a human.
02-mapping-your-processes.badExample: Make a flowchart of my process.
02-mapping-your-processes.goodExample: You are a business process analyst. Create a text-based flowchart for my content moderation workflow: (1) User submits content, (2) AI classifies as safe/borderline/unsafe, (3) If safe: auto-publish, (4) If borderline: queue for human review with AI's confidence score, (5) If unsafe: auto-reject with specific policy violation, (6) For human review: reviewer decides approve/reject/edit. Use [boxes] for actions, <diamonds> for decisions, -> for flow. Mark each node as AI, Human, or System.
02-mapping-your-processes.successMessage: Process maps with decision branches reveal exactly where AI fits — and where humans are still essential.

03-trigger-based-workflows.instruction: Design 3 trigger-based AI automations for a small business. For each, specify: the trigger event, any conditions, and 2-3 automated actions that follow.
03-trigger-based-workflows.hint: Think of events that happen regularly and always need the same follow-up actions. Good triggers: new form submission, payment received, review posted, email received, inventory threshold crossed, calendar event starting.
03-trigger-based-workflows.badExample: 1. Automate emails. 2. Automate social media. 3. Automate invoices.
03-trigger-based-workflows.goodExample: Automation 1: When a 1-star Google review is posted -> AI analyzes specific complaints -> drafts empathetic response addressing each concern -> auto-posts reply within 15 min + sends Slack alert to owner. Automation 2: When a customer invoice is 7 days overdue -> AI checks payment history (first vs. repeat offense) -> generates appropriate follow-up email (gentle vs. firm) -> auto-sends from billing address + logs in CRM. Automation 3: When product inventory drops below 20% of monthly average sales -> AI calculates optimal reorder quantity based on velocity + lead time -> generates purchase order -> emails supplier + alerts owner on Slack.
03-trigger-based-workflows.successMessage: Three automations that run while you sleep. The pattern: specific trigger + smart conditions + automatic actions.

03-trigger-based-workflows.instruction: A user built a trigger workflow: 'When a customer emails, AI auto-responds with a helpful answer.' But customers complain about irrelevant responses, duplicate replies, and the AI responding to internal team emails. Diagnose and fix each issue.
03-trigger-based-workflows.placeholder: Problem 1: Irrelevant responses — caused by... Fix: ...
03-trigger-based-workflows.hint: Three problems: (1) No business context — AI has no knowledge base. Fix: RAG with FAQs and product docs. (2) No thread deduplication — triggers on every email including replies. Fix: track by Message-ID, only trigger on new threads. (3) No sender filtering — responds to internal emails too. Fix: whitelist external domains, blacklist company domain.
03-trigger-based-workflows.badExample: The AI needs to be smarter.
03-trigger-based-workflows.goodExample: Three bugs, three fixes: (1) Irrelevant responses = no business context. AI generates from general knowledge, not company info. Fix: build RAG pipeline — index FAQs, product docs, resolved tickets. Only auto-reply when retrieval confidence > 0.85. (2) Duplicate replies = no thread awareness. Trigger fires on every email including replies within threads. Fix: track by Message-ID/In-Reply-To headers, only trigger on new conversations. (3) Internal emails = no sender filtering. Fix: blocklist company domain and vendor emails. Only process external/unknown senders.
03-trigger-based-workflows.successMessage: Most trigger workflow failures are missing filters, missing context, and missing deduplication — not the AI itself.

04-data-flow-design.instruction: Design a data flow for a form submission that needs to be routed to the right team. Show: what data is captured, how it's transformed, where it goes, and what notifications are sent.
04-data-flow-design.placeholder: Input: Form fields [name, email, type, message] -> Transform: AI classifies type...
04-data-flow-design.hint: Input: form fields (name, email, issue type, description). Transform: AI enriches with sentiment score, urgency classification, and suggested category. Route: billing issues to finance (Slack + ticket), technical bugs to engineering (Jira + Slack), feature requests to product (Notion). Store: all submissions in database with classification metadata. Notify: submitter gets acknowledgment email, assigned team gets Slack alert with AI summary.
04-data-flow-design.badExample: Form goes to the right team somehow.
04-data-flow-design.goodExample: Input: Contact form captures [name, email, category dropdown, message text]. Transform stage: (1) AI classifies urgency (low/medium/high/critical) from message content, (2) AI extracts key entities (product mentioned, account ID if included), (3) AI generates 2-sentence summary. Route: critical urgency -> PagerDuty + Slack #urgent. Billing category -> Finance team Zendesk queue. Technical -> Engineering Jira with AI-generated bug report. General -> Support inbox with suggested response draft. Store: PostgreSQL with all original fields + AI enrichment (urgency, entities, summary). Notify: submitter gets instant email with ticket number and estimated response time based on urgency.
04-data-flow-design.successMessage: Clean data flow prevents the #1 automation bug: wrong format, wrong place, wrong team.

04-data-flow-design.instruction: Analyze this data flow and identify potential problems: Customer form -> data sent directly to Claude API -> Claude's response saved to Google Sheet -> Zapier emails the analysis to the team. What could go wrong?
04-data-flow-design.hint: Issues: (1) No input validation (prompt injection, empty fields). (2) No error handling (Claude down = data lost). (3) Google Sheets as database (row limits, no concurrent writes). (4) Single point of failure at every step. (5) No rate limiting (bot spam). (6) Privacy risk (PII to Claude and Sheets with loose sharing).
04-data-flow-design.badExample: It looks fine, Google Sheets works.
04-data-flow-design.goodExample: Six issues: (1) No input validation — raw form data to LLM means prompt injection risk and garbage-in-garbage-out. Need schema validation + sanitization. (2) No queue/retry — if Claude is down, submissions are lost forever. Need a message queue that persists and retries. (3) Google Sheets as storage — 10M cell limit, can't handle concurrent writes, no real queries. Need PostgreSQL for primary storage. (4) Zapier single point of failure — if the Zap breaks, no emails and no alerts. Need delivery confirmation. (5) No rate limiting — forms can be spammed. Need CAPTCHA + per-IP limits. (6) PII exposure — customer data flows through 4 third-party services, each a breach vector.
04-data-flow-design.successMessage: Simple-looking data flows hide critical reliability and security problems. Every integration point is a potential failure.

05-error-handling-and-fallbacks.instruction: Your invoice automation workflow fails halfway: the invoice was created in your accounting system but the email notification didn't send. Design the complete error handling: detection, recovery, notification, and prevention.
05-error-handling-and-fallbacks.placeholder: Error detected when: ... Recovery: ... Notification: ... Prevention: ...
05-error-handling-and-fallbacks.hint: Detection: email API returns non-200 status or times out. Recovery: retry 3 times with exponential backoff (1s, 5s, 30s). If still fails: queue to dead letter queue, create a manual task for the team. Notification: alert operations team via Slack with invoice ID and error details. Prevention: add health check for email service before starting the workflow, implement circuit breaker pattern.
05-error-handling-and-fallbacks.badExample: If email fails, try again later.
05-error-handling-and-fallbacks.goodExample: Detection: email API returns 500 or times out after 10s. Immediate action: log {invoice_id, error_code, timestamp, attempt_number}. Recovery: retry 3x with backoff (2s, 10s, 60s). If email service is completely down (3 consecutive failures), activate circuit breaker — stop attempting emails for 5 minutes, check health endpoint. If all retries fail: (1) save email to 'pending_emails' queue in database, (2) create manual task in project management tool, (3) alert ops team on Slack: 'Invoice #1234 created but email failed after 3 retries. Manual send needed.' Prevention: pre-check email service health before starting workflow. If unhealthy, queue the entire workflow for later instead of creating a half-completed invoice.
05-error-handling-and-fallbacks.successMessage: Error handling separates demo automations from production systems. Every step needs a fallback.

05-error-handling-and-fallbacks.instruction:
Rewrite this fragile workflow to be resilient:
1. Fetch customer data from CRM
2. Send to Claude for analysis
3. Parse Claude's JSON response
4. Update CRM with insights
5. Send Slack notification

Add error handling, retries, fallbacks, and logging at each step.
05-error-handling-and-fallbacks.placeholder:
Resilient version:
1. Fetch CRM data → retry 3x, if down use cache...
05-error-handling-and-fallbacks.hint: Add at each step: retry logic, fallback behavior, data validation, and structured logging. Key additions: Redis cache for CRM data, fallback LLM if Claude is down, JSON repair for malformed responses, local storage if CRM write fails, and non-blocking Slack notifications.
05-error-handling-and-fallbacks.badExample: Just add try-catch around each step.
05-error-handling-and-fallbacks.goodExample: Resilient rewrite: 1. Fetch CRM → retry 3x (1s, 3s, 9s backoff). Fallback: Redis cache of last fetch (24h TTL). If both fail: log error, skip customer, continue. 2. Validate + sanitize data → check required fields, strip PII before sending to LLM. 3. Claude analysis → 30s timeout, 3 retries. Fallback: try Haiku if Sonnet fails. Dead letter queue if all LLMs fail. Log: {model, tokens, latency}. 4. Parse JSON → try/catch with json-repair library. If still broken: re-prompt with structured output mode. Log raw response on failure. 5. Update CRM → retry 3x. If fails: write to local SQLite backup, schedule background sync. Never lose insights. 6. Slack notification → 2 retries, 5s timeout. If fails: log but DO NOT block the workflow. This is informational, not critical.
05-error-handling-and-fallbacks.successMessage: Production-grade error handling. Every step has retries, fallbacks, logging, and a path forward when things break.

06-human-in-the-loop.instruction: Design a workflow where AI does the heavy lifting but humans approve critical steps. Define clearly what's autonomous (AI decides alone) versus what needs human approval, and why.
06-human-in-the-loop.placeholder: AI handles autonomously: ... Human approves: ... Because...
06-human-in-the-loop.hint: Think about risk levels. Low-risk, reversible actions can be autonomous. High-risk, irreversible, or customer-facing actions need human review. Example: AI can auto-categorize support tickets (low risk, easily corrected), but should not auto-send refunds over $100 without approval (high risk, irreversible).
06-human-in-the-loop.badExample: AI does everything automatically, humans check sometimes.
06-human-in-the-loop.goodExample: Content publishing workflow: AI autonomous: classify incoming content, generate SEO metadata, resize images, schedule to social media calendar. AI + human review: draft blog post (AI writes, human edits for voice/accuracy), generate email newsletter (AI drafts, human approves before send). Human only: crisis communications, policy statements, legal disclaimers. The dividing line: anything that's (a) public-facing, (b) irreversible, or (c) could harm brand reputation requires human approval. Everything internal, reversible, or low-stakes is autonomous.
06-human-in-the-loop.successMessage: The best workflows augment humans, not replace them. The key is matching autonomy level to risk level.

06-human-in-the-loop.instruction: A company requires human approval for ALL AI-generated customer emails, including simple order confirmations. Their review queue has a 4-hour backlog. Analyze: is this the right level of human oversight, and what should change?
06-human-in-the-loop.hint: Over-caution creating worse customer experience than occasional AI errors. Order confirmations are templated and data-driven — no creative AI content. 4-hour delay is worse than any plausible error. Solution: create tiers — auto-send for templated messages, spot-check 10% for personalized ones, require approval only for high-stakes communications.
06-human-in-the-loop.badExample: Just let AI send everything without review.
06-human-in-the-loop.goodExample: The 4-hour backlog causes more harm than unreviewed AI would. Fix with 3 tiers: Auto-send (no review): transactional messages — order confirmations, shipping updates, password resets. These are fill-in-the-blank templates, not generative content. ~60% of volume. Async audit (AI sends, humans review 10% randomly): personalized recommendations, follow-ups. Catch systematic issues without bottlenecks. ~30% of volume. Required approval: complaint responses, refund decisions, anything mentioning policies. ~10% of volume. Result: queue drops from 200+ to ~20 needing actual human judgment. Add quality scorecard to track if auto-sent emails cause any issues.
06-human-in-the-loop.successMessage: Excellent tiered analysis. Put humans where they add the most value — judgment on high-stakes decisions, not rubber-stamping templates.

07-integration-patterns.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to recommend integration patterns for connecting your CRM, email tool, and project management tool. Include the specific tools you use and what data needs to sync between them.
07-integration-patterns.placeholder: I use [CRM], [email tool], and [PM tool]. I need to sync...
07-integration-patterns.hint: Be specific about which tools and what data flows between them. Example: 'When a deal closes in HubSpot, create a project in Notion with the client details, and send an onboarding email sequence via Resend.' Specify direction of data flow, frequency of sync, and what triggers the integration.
07-integration-patterns.badExample: How do I connect my tools?
07-integration-patterns.goodExample: You are an integration architect. Connect Stripe (payments), Supabase (database), and Resend (email) into an automated lifecycle. When Stripe checkout.session.completed fires: (1) Edge Function creates/updates customer in Supabase with plan tier and dates, (2) Based on tier, send appropriate welcome email via Resend (different templates per tier). When customer.subscription.deleted fires: (1) Update user to 'churned' in Supabase, (2) Trigger 3-email win-back sequence (day 1, 3, 7). Include error handling for webhook verification and idempotency.
07-integration-patterns.successMessage: Integration architecture is the backbone of modern workflows. Webhooks + edge functions = real-time automation.

07-integration-patterns.instruction: Compare three integration approaches: (1) Direct API polling, (2) Webhooks, (3) Middleware like Make.com. For each, explain when it's the best choice and the main tradeoff.
07-integration-patterns.placeholder:
API Polling: Best when... Tradeoff: ...
Webhooks: Best when...
07-integration-patterns.hint: Polling: best when source doesn't support webhooks or you need batch processing. Tradeoff: wastes API calls, introduces latency. Webhooks: best for real-time event-driven flows. Tradeoff: need public endpoint, complex error handling, must handle retries/deduplication. Make.com: best for non-developers or rapid prototyping. Tradeoff: monthly cost at scale, limited customization, vendor lock-in.
07-integration-patterns.badExample: Webhooks are the best, just use those.
07-integration-patterns.goodExample: API Polling: Best when source has no webhook support or you need batch analytics (daily reports, periodic syncs). Tradeoff: wasted calls (90% polls return nothing), latency up to polling interval, risk of rate limits. Webhooks: Best for real-time triggers (new lead -> instant AI qualification). Tradeoff: need reliable public endpoint, must handle retries/deduplication, harder to debug. Make.com: Best for non-dev teams, rapid prototyping, connecting 5+ tools with standard connectors. Tradeoff: $29-99/month for useful plans, complex logic gets messy in visual builder, migration to code is manual. Recommendation: prototype with Make.com, migrate to webhooks + code for production volume.
07-integration-patterns.successMessage: The right pattern depends on latency needs, team skills, and volume. Many teams use all three for different workflows.

08-testing-workflows.instruction: Create a test plan for an automated workflow: 3 happy path tests, 2 edge case tests, and 1 failure scenario test. For each, define the specific input and the expected output.
08-testing-workflows.placeholder: Test 1 (happy path): Input: standard customer email... Expected: correctly categorized, response drafted...
08-testing-workflows.hint: Pick a specific workflow to test (email classification, form routing, invoice processing). Happy paths test normal operation. Edge cases test unusual but valid inputs (empty fields, non-English text, extremely long input). Failure scenarios test what happens when dependencies are down (API timeout, database unreachable).
08-testing-workflows.badExample: Test that it works and test that it handles errors.
08-testing-workflows.goodExample: Testing: AI email classification + auto-response workflow. Happy paths: (1) Input: 'I can't log into my account.' Expected: categorized as 'access_issue', response drafted with password reset link, routed to support queue. (2) Input: 'I'd like a refund for order #1234.' Expected: categorized as 'refund_request', response acknowledges order number, routed to billing. (3) Input: 'Your product is amazing!' Expected: categorized as 'feedback_positive', response thanks them, flagged for marketing testimonials. Edge cases: (4) Input: email body is empty, subject line only says 'Help.' Expected: categorized as 'unclear', routed to human review queue (not auto-responded). (5) Input: email in Spanish. Expected: language detected, response drafted in Spanish, flagged for multilingual review. Failure: (6) Claude API returns 500. Expected: email queued for retry, customer receives 'We received your message and will respond within 4 hours' template.
08-testing-workflows.successMessage: Testing before go-live prevents the 2 AM panic. Edge cases are where workflows actually break.

08-testing-workflows.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to design a monitoring strategy for catching AI workflow quality degradation over time. How would you detect if accuracy dropped from 95% to 80% without anyone noticing?
08-testing-workflows.placeholder: Design a quality monitoring system that detects...
08-testing-workflows.hint: You are a QA engineer. My AI classification workflow was 95% accurate at launch. Design monitoring to catch degradation. Include: golden test set (weekly automated tests), human audit sampling (5% of production), confidence score tracking, distribution drift detection, and downstream metric correlation.
08-testing-workflows.goodExample: You are a QA engineer for AI systems. Design a 5-layer quality monitoring system for my AI email classifier. (1) Weekly golden test: 200 labeled emails run through the pipeline — alert if accuracy drops below 90%. (2) Daily production sampling: 5% of classifications queued for human review, rolling 7-day accuracy tracked. (3) Confidence distribution: daily histogram of AI confidence scores — alert if median drops 5% or low-confidence volume exceeds 15%. (4) Category drift: if any category deviates 15% from 30-day average, flag for review. (5) Business metric correlation: track if support resolution time or CSAT correlates with classification changes. Output as a dashboard spec.
08-testing-workflows.successMessage: AI quality degrades silently. Without proactive monitoring, you won't know until customers complain.

09-monitoring-and-maintenance.instruction: Design a monitoring dashboard for your automated workflows. Include: key metrics to track, alert thresholds, and what 'healthy' vs 'unhealthy' looks like for each metric.
09-monitoring-and-maintenance.placeholder: Metric 1: Workflow success rate — Healthy: >98% — Alert when: <95%...
09-monitoring-and-maintenance.hint: Key metrics: success rate (>98% healthy), execution time (within 2x of baseline), error rate by type (API failures vs. logic errors), queue depth (growing = falling behind), cost per execution (staying within budget). Alerts: error rate >5% for 10 min, queue depth growing for 30 min, cost spike >200% of daily average.
09-monitoring-and-maintenance.badExample: Monitor if it's working or not.
09-monitoring-and-maintenance.goodExample: Dashboard with 5 metrics: (1) Success rate — Healthy: >98%. Warning: 95-98%. Critical: <95%. Action: check error logs for the failing step. (2) Execution time p95 — Healthy: <30s. Warning: 30-60s. Critical: >60s. Action: check for slow API calls or growing data volumes. (3) Queue depth — Healthy: <50 items. Warning: 50-200. Critical: >200 (falling behind). Action: check if a dependency is down or rate limited. (4) AI confidence average — Healthy: >0.8. Warning: 0.6-0.8. Critical: <0.6. Action: check for input data changes or model drift. (5) Daily cost — Healthy: within 20% of average. Warning: 20-50% over. Critical: >50% over. Action: check for traffic spike or prompt bloat. Alerts: Slack for warnings, PagerDuty for critical.
09-monitoring-and-maintenance.successMessage: You can't manage what you can't measure. These five metrics catch most workflow problems before users notice.

09-monitoring-and-maintenance.instruction: An AI workflow that classifies orders suddenly misclassifies 30% of orders as 'returns' when they're actually purchases. This started Tuesday morning. The AI model hasn't changed. What's your debugging process?
09-monitoring-and-maintenance.placeholder: Step 1: Check if input data format changed...
09-monitoring-and-maintenance.hint: Since the model didn't change, the data or context changed. Check: (1) Did the input format change Tuesday? Compare payloads. (2) Did anyone modify the prompt? Check git history. (3) Did the knowledge base/RAG context update? (4) Did a new data source or sales channel activate? (5) Did the LLM provider push a model update? Most likely: upstream data format change.
09-monitoring-and-maintenance.badExample: The AI is broken, retrain it.
09-monitoring-and-maintenance.goodExample: Debugging for Tuesday onset, no model change: (1) Diff Monday vs Tuesday payloads — sudden 30% misclassification without model changes almost always means input changed. Was the order form, CRM, or API schema modified? (2) Git blame the prompt — any changes pushed Monday night? (3) Check LLM provider changelog — silent model updates happen. Compare model version strings. (4) Sample 20 misclassified orders — what do they share? Same product? Same region? Same payment type? (5) Check if new return policy was added to knowledge base — RAG might surface return-related context for purchase queries. (6) Run Monday's orders through current pipeline — if they still classify correctly, it's the input data, not the model.
09-monitoring-and-maintenance.successMessage: Systematic debugging. When AI breaks without model changes, it's almost always the data or prompt that changed upstream.

10-your-workflow-portfolio.instruction: Create your Workflow Portfolio: 5 AI workflows ordered by impact. For each: name, trigger event, what it automates, and estimated time saved per week.
10-your-workflow-portfolio.placeholder:
Workflow 1 (highest impact): Smart Email Triage
Trigger: New email arrives
Automates: Classification, priority scoring, response drafting
Time saved: 4 hours/week
10-your-workflow-portfolio.hint: Rank by time saved per week. The best first workflow saves the most time with the simplest implementation. Good categories: communication (email, messages), reporting (status updates, analytics), content (social media, documentation), operations (invoicing, scheduling), research (competitive analysis, trend monitoring).
10-your-workflow-portfolio.badExample: 1. Email automation. 2. Reporting. 3. Social media. 4. Scheduling. 5. Data entry.
10-your-workflow-portfolio.goodExample: 1. Client Proposal Drafter — Trigger: lead marked 'qualified' in CRM. Automates: generates customized proposal from intake form + past similar proposals. Time saved: 10 hours/week (3 hours -> 15 min per proposal x 4/week). 2. Weekly Status Report — Trigger: every Friday 2PM. Automates: pulls from Jira + Slack + GitHub, generates executive summary. Time saved: 2 hours/week. 3. Social Content Engine — Trigger: daily at 6AM + blog publish. Automates: generates platform-specific posts from blog/news. Time saved: 6 hours/week. 4. Meeting Notes Processor — Trigger: recording uploaded. Automates: transcription + action items + Slack summary. Time saved: 3 hours/week. 5. Expense Categorizer — Trigger: new bank transaction. Automates: AI categorizes, matches invoices, flags anomalies. Time saved: 3.5 hours/week. Total: 24.5 hours/week reclaimed.
10-your-workflow-portfolio.successMessage: A prioritized workflow portfolio is a roadmap to operational freedom. Start with #1 and work down.

10-your-workflow-portfolio.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to create an implementation roadmap for your top 3 workflows. Include your technical skills, available tools, and time constraints.
10-your-workflow-portfolio.placeholder: Help me create a roadmap to implement these 3 workflows...
10-your-workflow-portfolio.hint: Include specific constraints: your technical ability (can you code or need no-code?), tools you already have (Make.com, Supabase, etc.), hours per week available for building, and which workflow should go first. Ask AI to estimate build time and suggest shared infrastructure.
10-your-workflow-portfolio.badExample: Which workflow should I build first?
10-your-workflow-portfolio.goodExample: You are a workflow automation consultant. Prioritize and roadmap these 3 workflows for me: (1) Email triage — 4 hrs/week saved, (2) Meeting notes processor — 3 hrs/week, (3) Weekly report generator — 2 hrs/week. My skills: basic JavaScript, can use APIs. Tools: Make.com free tier, Supabase, Claude API. Time: 5 hours/week for building. For each: estimated build time, specific tools needed, dependencies on other workflows. I want the first workflow live within 2 weeks. Which workflows share infrastructure (email processing, AI calls) so building one makes the next easier?
10-your-workflow-portfolio.successMessage: A solid roadmap turns workflow ideas into reality. Prioritize by ROI and build shared infrastructure first.
